one_line_takeaway: In-processing generative watermarks (StableSignature, Tree-Ring, Gaussian Shading) that resist conventional signal-level attacks can have detectability collapse under semantically meaningful edits (especially StableSignature, which drops to near-chance bit accuracy across all semantic-edit tiers), revealing that current watermark robustness benchmarks miss a critical, realistic threat vector.
key_finding: Under the improved image-processing baselines (Sec. 6.3), all three watermarks remain largely robust: StableSignature survives seam carving even after removing nearly half the seams, and block shuffling has little effect on it, though row occlusion and impulse noise drop its bit accuracy to 50-60%; Tree-Ring and Gaussian Shading generally retain acceptable detectability but are notably degraded by seam carving and (unlike StableSignature) by block shuffling. Under semantic perturbations (Sec. 6.4, Table 1), the picture changes sharply and non-uniformly across methods. StableSignature collapses to near-chance bit accuracy (49-51%) across all three local perturbation tiers (texture shift, intra-class, inter-class) and drops to 53-65% under global stylization -- i.e., its watermark is effectively erased by semantic edits regardless of edit severity or region size (confirmed in Fig. 6b, where detectability stays near-chance even for small masks). Tree-Ring shows a distinct pattern: under local edits it remains statistically detectable but with much weaker evidence (p-values rising from ~1e-7 to the 1e-7-1e-6 range across variants), and detectability degrades monotonically with the fraction of the image replaced (Fig. 6a); under global stylization its p-values rise further into the 1e-3-1e-1 range, approaching or crossing typical non-detection thresholds. Gaussian Shading is the most robust of the three, maintaining bit accuracy >=98% under both local and global semantic perturbations with no clear dependence on mask size (Fig. 6c). A cross-family experiment (Table 2, using Kandinsky 2.1 as an inpainter distinct from the SD v2-1 watermarked model) shows cross-family edits degrade detection metrics more than same-family edits for all three schemes (e.g., StableSignature bit accuracy 50.10% same-family vs. 53.49% cross-family; Tree-Ring p-value 3.88e-07 vs. 9.08e-07; Gaussian Shading 99.60% vs. 98.43%), but typically do not eliminate the watermark completely.
core_contribution: The paper (Nakra and Wu, UMD) introduces a modular, black-box, multi-stage framework for stress-testing in-processing generative image watermarks (StableSignature, Tree-Ring, Gaussian Shading) against semantic perturbations rather than conventional pixel/signal-level attacks. The framework has two stages: (1) Mask R-CNN instance segmentation to localize the most salient object in a watermarked image, and (2) text-guided diffusion inpainting (or, for global shifts, VGG-based neural style transfer) to regenerate that region with altered semantic content, formalized as Psem(xw,M,c) = (1-M)⊙xw + M⊙Inpaint(xw,M,c). Local edits are graded into three tiers of 'semantic gap': intra-class texture shift, intra-class object replacement, and inter-class substitution. The paper also contributes an expanded suite of image-processing baselines (seam carving, downsample/upsample, impulse noise, row occlusion/interleaving, morphological erosion/dilation, partial and complete block shuffling) that had not previously been systematically applied to these three watermarking schemes. Extensive experiments on MS-COCO 2017 (5,000 validation images, Stable Diffusion v2-1 backbone) show that watermark robustness under semantic edits does not track robustness under conventional perturbations: schemes that survive a broad suite of signal-level attacks can have detectability collapse to near-chance under semantic drift, while visual/perceptual quality (SSIM) remains comparatively high.
threat_model: Formal black-box adversary: given only a watermarked image xw = G(z,s) (no access to signature s, generator G, or detector D parameters), the adversary applies a semantic perturbation operator Psem using off-the-shelf public tools (an object detector/segmenter plus a text-guided inpainting or style-transfer model) to produce x' = Psem(xw,M,c) that reduces watermark detectability while preserving perceptual fidelity and scene plausibility. This models a realistic Model-as-a-Service (MaaS) setting where users have only query-level access and may not even know which watermarking scheme protects the content. The paper also evaluates a cross-family variant where the inpainting model (Kandinsky 2.1) differs from the watermarked model's generator family (Stable Diffusion v2-1), reflecting that adversaries may use editing tools unrelated to the original generator.
limitations: The paper explicitly notes (Sec. 6.2) that full-reference fidelity metrics (PSNR, SSIM) can be misleading for semantically edited images because global stylization intentionally alters style/content and would be unfairly penalized by these metrics, motivating their caption-based BLIPA/VLMA proxies -- but those proxies are themselves imperfect (BLIP captions noted to include spurious metadata). The evaluation is limited to three representative in-processing schemes and a single base generator (Stable Diffusion v2-1); generalization to other architectures/schemes is not empirically tested. The paper is explicitly framed as a 'first-step effort' (Sec. 7) that identifies and quantifies the vulnerability but does not provide a mechanistic explanation for why semantic manipulation affects different watermarking schemes so unevenly -- this is left as future work. Object selection for local edits is limited to the single largest-area detected instance per image, which may not capture all realistic edit scenarios. No adaptive/white-box adversary is considered; the threat model is strictly black-box with off-the-shelf tools.

datasets_used: MS-COCO 2017 validation split (5,000 captioned images, resized to 512x512)
DiffusionDB / Stable Diffusion Prompts dataset (used to source inpainting edit prompts)
quantitative_results: StableSignature bit accuracy under global stylization: 57.8% +/- 6.0%
StableSignature bit accuracy under local texture shift: 50.2% +/- 7.9%
StableSignature bit accuracy under local intra-class replacement: 49.5% +/- 7.6%
StableSignature bit accuracy under local inter-class substitution: 49.9% +/- 7.9%
Tree-Ring average p-value under global stylization: 2.7e-02
Tree-Ring average p-value under local texture shift: 1.4e-07
Tree-Ring average p-value under local intra-class replacement: 1.5e-06
Tree-Ring average p-value under local inter-class substitution: 8.8e-08
Gaussian Shading bit accuracy under global stylization: 98.9% +/- 0.2%
Gaussian Shading bit accuracy under local perturbations: 99.6-99.7% (>=98% overall per authors' summary)

Cross-family (Kandinsky 2.1) vs same-family local edits (Table 2): StableSignature bit accuracy 50.10% (same) vs 53.49% (cross); Tree-Ring p-value 3.88e-07 (same) vs 9.08e-07 (cross); Gaussian Shading bit accuracy 99.60% (same) vs 98.43% (cross)
Baseline seam-carving (50% seams removed) bit accuracy: StableSignature 0.96, Gaussian Shading 0.68, Tree-Ring p-value 1.80e-02
Baseline impulse noise (p=0.5) bit accuracy: StableSignature 0.55, Gaussian Shading 0.85, Tree-Ring p-value 1.63e-02
Baseline complete block shuffling (block size 4) bit accuracy: StableSignature 0.47, Gaussian Shading 0.49, Tree-Ring p-value 4.75e-01 (near non-detection)
baselines_compared: StableSignature (Fernandez et al., decoder fine-tuning watermark)
Tree-Ring (Wen et al., Fourier-domain initial-noise watermark)
Gaussian Shading (Yang et al., encrypted-bitstring latent-sampling watermark)
